body.purpose: Default text for assistant messages, tool details, and markdown content.

heading.purpose: All markdown headings H1-H6. No size variation; BOLD attribute only.

label.purpose: Status text, timestamps, metadata, keyboard hints. Always DIM.

shadows.name: none
shadows.purpose: No shadows. The terminal is a flat medium; depth comes from tonal layering.

shadows.name: backdrop-dim
shadows.purpose: Semi-transparent black wash behind overlays. Creates depth separation for modals.

motion.name: none
motion.purpose: No transitions. The terminal updates synchronously; no motion choreography.

components.name: Header
components.description: Top bar with bold pink GAMBIT logo, version, timestamp, and workspace path.
components.css: .ds-header { display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; padding: 4px 8px; background: #181818; font-family: 'Cascadia Code', 'Fira Code', monospace; font-size: 13px; } .ds-header-logo { color: #FFB6C1; font-weight: 700; } .ds-header-time { color: #4D4E4E; opacity: 0.6; } .ds-header-path { color: #4D4E4E; opacity: 0.6; }

components.name: Composer
components.description: Rounded-border input box with pink › prompt. The primary interaction surface.
components.html: ›Ask anything or @ tag files/folders
components.css: .ds-composer { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 4px; padding: 4px 8px; background: #131313; border: 1px solid #222222; border-radius: 6px; font-family: 'Cascadia Code', 'Fira Code', monospace; font-size: 13px; } .ds-composer-prompt { color: #FFB6C1; font-weight: 700; } .ds-composer-placeholder { color: #4D4E4E; } .ds-composer:focus-within { border-color: #FFB6C1; }

components.name: Message - Assistant
components.description: Assistant response on the canvas background. Cool silver text recedes into the dark.
components.html:
Assistant

Let me look at the theme system to understand the full color palette.
components.css: .ds-msg-assistant { padding: 4px 16px; background: #131313; font-family: 'Cascadia Code', 'Fira Code', monospace; font-size: 13px; } .ds-msg-label { color: #4D4E4E; opacity: 0.6; font-size: 11px; } .ds-msg-body { margin: 4px 0 0; color: #C9D1D9; line-height: 1.5; }

components.name: Message - User
components.description: User input on a slightly raised background. White text for clarity.
components.html:
You

document the design system
components.css: .ds-msg-user { padding: 4px 16px; background: #1B1B1B; font-family: 'Cascadia Code', 'Fira Code', monospace; font-size: 13px; } .ds-msg-label { color: #4D4E4E; opacity: 0.6; font-size: 11px; } .ds-msg-body { margin: 4px 0 0; color: #FFFFFF; line-height: 1.5; }

components.name: Message - Tool
components.description: Tool call with purple-tinted background. Pink text and bullet signal a tool action.
components.css: .ds-msg-tool { padding: 2px 16px; background: #321F33; font-family: 'Cascadia Code', 'Fira Code', monospace; font-size: 13px; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 4px; } .ds-msg-bullet { color: #7EE787; font-weight: 700; } .ds-msg-tool-name { color: #FFB6C1; font-weight: 700; } .ds-msg-tool-detail { color: #4D4E4E; opacity: 0.6; }

components.name: Reasoning Block
components.description: Collapsible internal monologue. Solid left bar in pink. The signature visual pattern.
components.html:
+Thought: 2.3s

Analyzing the theme tokens to extract the color palette and spacing system...
components.css: .ds-reasoning { border-left: 3px solid #FFB6C1; padding: 4px 12px; background: #1C1C1C; font-family: 'Cascadia Code', 'Fira Code', monospace; font-size: 13px; } .ds-reasoning-toggle { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 4px; } .ds-reasoning-marker { color: #FFB6C1; font-weight: 700; } .ds-reasoning-label { color: #FFB6C1; font-weight: 700; } .ds-reasoning-duration { color: #4D4E4E; opacity: 0.6; } .ds-reasoning-body { margin: 4px 0 0; color: #FFB6C1; line-height: 1.5; }

components.name: Permission Prompt
components.description: High-stakes modal with heavy left border. Pink title, white subject, dim hints.
components.html:
Permission Required

bash: bun run src/index.tsx

Y/Enter to allow · N/Esc to deny · Shift+Tab to change mode
components.css: .ds-permission-backdrop { display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; padding: 32px 16px; background: rgba(0,0,0,0.588); font-family: 'Cascadia Code', 'Fira Code', monospace; font-size: 13px; } .ds-permission { border-left: 4px solid #808080; padding: 8px 16px; background: #181818; max-width: 420px; display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 8px; } .ds-permission-title { color: #FFB6C1; font-weight: 700; } .ds-permission-subject { margin: 0; color: #FFFFFF; } .ds-permission-hints { color: #4D4E4E; opacity: 0.6; font-size: 11px; }

narrative.overview:
Gambit is a confident, opinionated tool that's not afraid to be itself. The pink accent (#FFB6C1) is a signature, not a decoration — it says "this tool has personality" without shouting. Like a well-themed editor that feels alive. The terminal is the medium; Gambit embraces its constraints rather than fighting them.

This is a product register: design serves the developer's workflow. Every visual element earns its pixels by carrying signal — status, state, role, focus. The system rejects three things explicitly: generic terminal drab (dense monochrome walls with no visual hierarchy), web-app mimicry (fake shadows, pseudo-windows, rounded boxes fighting the character grid), and over-designed SaaS-in-terminal (too many colors, bloated chrome wasting terminal rows on decoration).

The system ships 13 themes, but only two are Gambit's own: gambit-dark (the default) and gambit-light. The remaining 11 are familiar editor themes offered as user-selectable alternatives. The brand identity lives in the Gambit themes; the others are courtesy.
keyCharacteristics: Monospace-native: every element aligns to the character grid; no fractional positioning
Single-accent discipline: Blossom (#FFB6C1) is the only brand color, used for focus, prompts, borders, and active states
Tonal depth: background → panel → header creates hierarchy through 1-2 step luminance shifts, not shadows
Attribute-driven hierarchy: BOLD for emphasis, DIM for de-emphasis; no font-size variation (terminal cells are uniform)
Tactile components: rounded borders, accent-colored prompts (›), visible state indicators

rules.name: The One Accent Rule
rules.body: Blossom (#FFB6C1) is the only brand color. It appears on focus, prompts, active selection, reasoning, and the logo — never as a background fill, never as decoration. If a new surface needs color, ask whether it's signaling state. If not, it doesn't get Blossom.

rules.name: The Warm Warning Rule
rules.body: In gambit-dark, warning uses Blossom rather than a separate yellow/amber. This is intentional — Gambit's warnings are warm, not alarming. Do not introduce a separate warning hue unless the theme explicitly defines one.

rules.name: The Ash Floor Rule
rules.body: Ash (#4D4E4E) is the quietest text that remains readable. Any text dimmer than Ash is invisible against Ink and is prohibited. If something needs to be de-emphasized further, remove it — don't dim it into nothing.

rules.name: The Attribute-Only Hierarchy Rule
rules.body: Never introduce a second font family. Hierarchy is BOLD > regular > DIM, in that order. If something needs more emphasis than BOLD, add color (Blossom). If it needs less than regular, add DIM. There is no fourth tier.

rules.name: The Monospace Alignment Rule
rules.body: Every element must align to the character grid. Tables, code blocks, diff views, and message padding all respect cell boundaries. Never use proportional spacing, fractional positions, or sub-cell rendering.

rules.name: The No-Shadow Rule
rules.body: Never use CSS box-shadow, text-shadow, or any shadow-like effect. The terminal is a flat medium. Depth comes from background color and border weight, never from simulated light.

rules.name: The Border-Weight Ladder Rule
rules.body: Border styles encode importance: rounded for the composer, heavy for permission prompts, single-line for standard containers, and custom ▌ left-border for reasoning blocks. Never use the same border style for two different importance levels.

dos: Do use Blossom (#FFB6C1) only for focus, prompts, active selection, reasoning, and the logo. Its rarity is its power.
Do convey hierarchy through background tonal shifts (Ink → Panel → Header Surface) — never through shadows.
Do use BOLD for emphasis and DIM for de-emphasis. These are the only two attribute tiers besides regular.
Do pair every color with a text label, icon, or position cue. Never rely on color alone to convey meaning.
Do use the › character as the universal focus/selection marker in pickers and the composer prompt.
Do use the ▌ left-border character for reasoning blocks — it's the signature visual pattern.
Do maintain the 3-step tonal ladder: Ink (#131313) → Panel (#141414) → Header Surface (#181818).
Do color-code context usage by threshold: Ash <60%, Warning 60-85%, Error >85%.
Do keep the Ash floor (#4D4E4E) as the minimum readable text color.
Do embrace the character grid — every element aligns to terminal cells.
donts: Don't introduce a second brand color. Blossom is the only accent.
Don't use CSS box-shadow, text-shadow, or any shadow simulation.
Don't create generic terminal drab — dense monochrome walls with no visual hierarchy.
Don't mimic web-app layouts — pseudo-windows, fake shadows, rounded boxes fighting the character grid.
Don't over-design with SaaS-in-terminal patterns — too many colors, bloated chrome wasting terminal rows.
Don't use Blossom as a background fill. It is always foreground, border, or accent.
Don't introduce a second font family. Monospace is the only family.
Don't use border-left greater than 1px as a colored stripe — the ▌ reasoning border is a custom character pattern.
Don't dim text below Ash (#4D4E4E). If it needs to be quieter, remove it.
Don't add the same border style to two different importance levels.
